title: Stationarity: The Stable Baseline
concept: Stationarity means that a time series has statistical properties — like its mean and variance — that remain constant over time, making it much easier for models to predict.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What makes seasonality distinct from cyclical patterns?

options.text: Seasonality repeats at fixed, predictable calendar intervals, while cyclical periods vary.
Seasonality only goes upward over time.
Cyclical patterns have constant zero entropy.

exercises.explain: Correct! Seasonality occurs at fixed intervals like weeks or months, whereas cyclical periods are irregular.

cards.title: Shooting at a moving target
cards.body: Imagine playing darts. If the board is bolted to the wall, you can practice, learn its distance, and hit the bullseye. But what if the board is constantly drifting upward and vibrating wildly? To make consistent predictions, statistical models require the target to stand still. We call this stationarity.

cards.title: Stationarity: The stable sequence
cards.body: A time series is **stationary** if its statistical properties do not change over time. Specifically, it has a **constant mean** (no upward/downward trend) and a **constant variance** (no shifting volatility). If the data has a trend, it is **non-stationary**.

cards.title: A calm lake vs. an incoming tide
cards.body: Think of a stationary series like a calm lake. Waves ripple up and down, but the average water level (mean) and height of the ripples (variance) stay constant. A non-stationary series is like an incoming tide on a beach: the baseline water level is steadily rising, changing the environment completely.

cards.title: Achieving stationarity: Differencing
cards.body: If daily sales rise steadily (non-stationary), we can't easily model them with simple statistics. But if we subtract yesterday's sales from today's sales, we get the **daily change**. This list of changes has no trend — it bounces around a constant mean of 0. This trick is called **differencing**.

cards.title: Stationary doesn't mean flat or dead
cards.body: You might assume a stationary series must be a straight, flat line. In reality, stationary data can have significant fluctuations and autocorrelation. It just means the *rules of the game* (mean and variance) don't change as time rolls forward, allowing the model to generalize.

left.text:
Constant average value.
Constant spread of fluctuations.
Predictable statistics.

right.text:
Average value drifts up or down.
Spread increases/decreases over time.
Unstable baseline.

exercises.prompt: What does it mean for a time series to be stationary?

options.text: Its statistical properties like mean and variance remain constant over time.
Its values never change and represent a straight line.
It can only contain text labels.

exercises.explain: Correct! Stationarity means constant statistical baseline properties (mean, variance) over time.

exercises.prompt: True or false: A time series with a strong upward trend is considered stationary.
exercises.explain: False! A trend means the average value (mean) is shifting over time, which makes the series non-stationary.

exercises.sentence: To make a trended series stationary, we calculate the difference between consecutive points, a process called ___.
exercises.explain: Differencing removes trends by modeling the changes between time steps rather than the absolute values.

options.text: Daily temperature changes (Today minus Yesterday) bouncing around a mean of 0° with stable variance.
Global carbon emissions rising steadily year-over-year for 50 years.
Stock market prices showing high growth and expanding volatility over a decade.

exercises.explain: Correct! Bouncing around a constant mean with stable variance is a stationary series.

exercises.prompt: You are preparing a monthly sales dataset for a forecasting model. The raw sales have a strong upward trend. What preprocessing step should you perform, and why?

options.text: Apply differencing (subtracting consecutive months) to remove the trend and achieve stationarity, making the baseline stable for modeling.
Use K-Means clustering to group months by sales volume.
Add a rolling average of 12 months without differencing.
Nothing, since linear models prefer non-stationary data.

exercises.explain: Exactly! Differencing removes the non-stationary trend, producing a stable, constant mean that is far easier for statistical forecasting models to learn and predict. Next, let's look at the ultimate state of random, unmodelable data: White Noise!
